The following is the input: Develop a weather forecast application using Flask. The application must have an endpoint GET /forecast which requires parameters 'city' and 'date' (in YYYY-MM-DD format). Use an external API like OpenWeatherMap to fetch the forecast data. Secure the endpoint with OAuth 2.0, where each request must include a valid access token, or return HTTP 403 (Forbidden). Integrate Redis to cache responses for each city for up to 2 hours to reduce API requests. Additionally, handle exceptions gracefully, providing meaningful error messages and appropriate HTTP status codes.

```python
from flask import Flask, jsonify, request
import requests
import redis
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

app = Flask(__name__)

# Redis configuration
redis_client = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0)

# OpenWeatherMap API key
API_KEY = 'your_openweathermap_api_key'

# Cache timeout in seconds
CACHE_TTL = 7200  # 2 hours

@app.route('/forecast', methods=['GET'])
def forecast():
    city = request.args.get('city')
    date = request.args.get('date')

    if not city or not date:
        return jsonify({"error": "Missing city or date parameter"}), 400

    # Validate date format
    if not date.strftime('%Y-%m-%d') == date:
        return jsonify({"error": "Invalid date format"}), 400

    # Check cache
    cached_data = redis_client.get(f'forecast:{city}:{date}')
    if cached_data:
        return jsonify(json.loads(cached_data)), 200

    # Fetch data from API
    url = f"http://api.openweathermap.org/data/2.5/forecast?q={city}&appid={API_KEY}&units=metric&date={date}"
    response = requests.get(url)
    response.raise_for_status()

    data = response.json()

    # Store in cache
    redis_client.setex(f'forecast:{city}:{date}', CACHE_TTL, json.dumps(data))
    return jsonify(data), 200

if __name__ == '__main__':
    app.run(debug=True)
```

The following is the input: You are tasked to develop a web-based task management system using Vue.js. Design and implement a feature where users can create, edit, and delete tasks. Each task should have a title, description, priority level (High, Medium, Low), and a deadline date. Integrate Vuex for state management and Vuetify for the UI components. Ensure there is an interactive calendar view where users can drag and drop tasks to reschedule. Additionally, tasks should be color-coded based on priority. Include error handling and validation to ensure that a task cannot be saved without a title or a deadline.

```vue
<template>
  <v-container>
    <v-card>
      <v-card-title>
        Task Management System
      </v-card-title>
      <v-card-text>
        <v-form @submit.prevent="saveTask">
          <v-row>
            <v-col cols="12">
              <v-text-field
                v-model="task.title"
                label="Title"
                required
              ></v-text-field>
            </v-col>
            <v-col cols="12">
              <v-text-field
                v-model="task.description"
                label="Description"
              ></v-text-field>
            </v-col>
            <v-col cols="12">
              <v-select
                v-model="task.priority"
                :items="['High', 'Medium', 'Low']"
                label="Priority"
                required
              ></v-select>
            </v-col>
            <v-col cols="12">
              <v-date-picker
                v-model="task.deadline"
                label="Deadline"
                required
              ></v-date-picker>
            </v-col>
          </v-row>
          <v-btn type="submit" color="primary">Save Task</v-btn>
        </v-form>
      </v-card-text>
    </v-card>
  </v-container>
</template>

<script>
export default {
  data() {
    return {
      task: {
        title: '',
        description: '',
        priority: '',
        deadline: null
      }
    };
  },
  methods: {
    saveTask() {
      if (!this.task.title || !this.task.deadline) {
        alert("Please fill in all required fields.");
        return;
      }
      // Here you would call an API or Vuex action to save the task
      console.log("Task saved:", this.task);
    }
  }
};
</script>
```

The following is the input: You are a beginner learning Python and are interested in basic data handling. Write a Python script that reads a CSV file containing employee data with columns "Name", "Age", and "Department". Your task is to filter out the records where the "Age" is above 30 and save these filtered records into a new CSV file. Additionally, print the count of these records.

import csv

# Read the CSV file
with open('input.csv', 'r') as file:
    reader = csv.DictReader(file)
    data = [row for row in reader]

# Filter records where Age is above 30
filtered_data = [row for row in data if int(row['Age']) > 30]

# Save filtered data to a new CSV file
with open('filtered.csv', 'w', newline='') as file:
    writer = csv.DictWriter(file, fieldnames=['Name', 'Age', 'Department'])
    writer.writeheader()
    writer.writerows(filtered_data)

# Print the count of filtered records
print(len(filtered_data))

The following is the input: You are tasked with developing an educational language learning app with a focus on Python and using Django. Create a Django application that features a word pronunciation assessment tool. Implement a feature where users can input a word and the application utilizes a speech recognition API to evaluate their pronunciation accuracy. The app should provide feedback and scoring based on the accuracy of the pronunciation, compare user results against standard pronunciations, and show historical progress. The application should be simple, intuitive, and ensure data privacy for user inputs.

from django.db import models
import speech_recognition as sr

class Word(models.Model):
    word = models.CharField(max_length=100)
    pronunciation = models.TextField()
    user_pronunciation = models.TextField()
    accuracy = models.FloatField(default=0.0)
    created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)

    def save(self, *args, **kwargs):
        # Use speech recognition to evaluate pronunciation
        r = sr.Recognizer()
        with sr.AudioFile(self.user_pronunciation) as source:
            audio_data = r.record(source)
            try:
                user_pronunciation_text = r.recognize_google(audio_data)
                self.accuracy = self.calculate_accuracy(self.pronunciation, user_pronunciation_text)
            except sr.UnknownValueError:
                self.accuracy = 0.0
            super().save(*args, **kwargs)

    def calculate_accuracy(self, standard, user):
        # Calculate accuracy based on user's pronunciation
        return sum(1 for a, b in zip(standard, user) if a == b) / len(standard)

class UserProgress(models.Model):
    user = models.ForeignKey('auth.User', on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    word = models.ForeignKey(Word, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    progress = models.FloatField(default=0.0)
    last_updated = models.DateTimeField(auto_now=True)

# Ensure data privacy by not storing user data in the database (implementation depends on backend)

The following is the input: Develop a Python script using the pandas library that reads weather data from a CSV file named "weather_data.csv", which includes daily temperature measurements for multiple cities. Your script should process this data to find the average temperature per city and then visually represent these averages in a bar chart using Matplotlib. Make sure to label each axis properly and provide a title for the chart. Additionally, ensure the bars are colored differently for temperatures in different ranges: cooler (<15°C), moderate (15-25°C), and warm (>25°C).

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Load the CSV file
df = pd.read_csv('weather_data.csv')

# Calculate average temperature per city
avg_temp = df.groupby('city')['temperature'].mean()

# Create a bar chart
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
avg_temp.plot(kind='bar', color=['blue' if t < 15 else 'green' if 15 <= t < 25 else 'red' for t in avg_temp])

# Label the axes and add title
plt.xlabel('City')
plt.ylabel('Average Temperature (°C)')
plt.title('Average Temperature by City')

# Show the plot
plt.show()

The following is the input: You are tasked with developing an API for a healthcare management system using Django and Django REST Framework. Implement an API endpoint PUT /patients/{id} to update an existing patient's records. The endpoint should accept fields such as name, age, medical_conditions (a list), and medications (a list), and update only the fields provided in the request. Use Django ORM for database operations and ensure proper error handling, such as returning HTTP 404 for non-existing patients and HTTP 400 for validation errors. Secure this endpoint with OAuth2, requiring an access token for authentication.

from rest_framework import viewsets, status
from rest_framework.response import Response
from rest_framework.exceptions import ValidationError
from rest_framework.authentication import TokenAuthentication
from rest_framework.permissions import IsAuthenticated
from django.db import models
from django.core.exceptions import ObjectDoesNotExist
from .models import Patient
from .serializers import PatientSerializer

class PatientViewSet(viewsets.ModelViewSet):
    queryset = Patient.objects.all()
    serializer_class = PatientSerializer
    permission_classes = [IsAuthenticated]
    authentication_classes = [TokenAuthentication]

    def update(self, request, *args, **kwargs):
        instance = self.get_object()
        data = request.data

        # Validate data
        try:
            self.serializer_class(instance, data=data, partial=True)
            self.serializer_class.update(instance, data=data)
            self.serializer_class.save()
            return Response(self.serializer_class.instance, status=status.HTTP_200_OK)
        except ValidationError as e:
            return Response({"error": str(e)}, status=status.HTTP_400_BAD_REQUEST)
        except ObjectDoesNotExist:
            return Response({"error": "Patient not found"}, status=status.HTTP_404_NOT_FOUND)

The following is the input: You are assigned to develop an integration feature for a health monitoring system, which communicates with various wearable devices APIs. Your specific task is to write Python code using Flask to create an API endpoint GET /api/devices/data that fetches and aggregates data from a specified set of device APIs (e.g., Fitbit, Garmin). The endpoint must accept parameters for the device type and the date range of data to fetch. Additionally, implement caching mechanisms using Redis to optimize response times and handle proper error responses for cases where device data is unavailable or the request parameters are invalid.

from flask import Flask, jsonify, request
import redis
import datetime
import json

app = Flask(__name__)
redis_client = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0)

# Cache key format: device_type_date_range
CACHE_KEY_FORMAT = "device:{device_type}:{date_start}:{date_end}"

@app.route('/api/devices/data', methods=['GET'])
def get_device_data():
    device_type = request.args.get('device_type')
    date_start = request.args.get('date_start')
    date_end = request.args.get('date_end')

    # Validate parameters
    if not device_type or not date_start or not date_end:
        return jsonify({"error": "Missing required parameters: device_type, date_start, date_end"}), 400

    try:
        date_start = datetime.datetime.strptime(date_start, "%Y-%m-%d")
        date_end = datetime.datetime.strptime(date_end, "%Y-%m-%d")
    except ValueError:
        return jsonify({"error": "Invalid date format. Use YYYY-MM-DD"}), 400

    # Check if data is cached
    cache_key = CACHE_KEY_FORMAT.format(
        device_type=device_type,
        date_start=date_start.strftime("%Y-%m-%d"),
        date_end=date_end.strftime("%Y-%m-%d")
    )
    cached_data = redis_client.get(cache_key)

    if cached_data:
        return jsonify(json.loads(cached_data)), 200

    # Simulate API call to device
    # In real scenario, this would be an actual API call
    data = {
        "device_type": device_type,
        "date_range": {
            "start": date_start.strftime("%Y-%m-%d"),
            "end": date_end.strftime("%Y-%m-%d")
        },
        "data": {
            "heart_rate": 72,
            "steps": 10000,
            "calories": 250
        }
    }

    # Cache the response
    redis_client.setex(cache_key, 3600, json.dumps(data))
    return jsonify(data), 200

if __name__ == '__main__':
    app.run(debug=True)

The following is the input: You are tasked with developing a customer feedback analysis module for a large retail enterprise. Implement a Python microservice using Django. The microservice should accept POST requests at /feedback containing JSON data with fields customer_id (integer, required), feedback_text (string, required), and feedback_date (ISO 8601 date string, optional). Use natural language processing (NLP) to analyze the sentiment of the feedback text and store the results along with the original data in a MySQL database. Ensure the application is secure, use HTTPS, and validate all inputs using Django forms.

from django.http import JsonResponse
from django.views import View
from django.forms.models import model_to_dict
from django.db import transaction
from datetime import datetime
import json
import re
from nltk.sentiment import SentimentIntensityAnalyzer
from nltk.corpus import vader_lexicon
from nltk.sentiment import SentimentIntensityAnalyzer
import mysql.connector

# Initialize the sentiment analyzer
nlp = SentimentIntensityAnalyzer()
vader_lexicon = vader_lexicon

# Database connection
db = mysql.connector.connect(
    host="localhost",
    user="your_db_user",
    password="your_db_password",
    database="feedback_db"
)

# Create a cursor
cursor = db.cursor()

class FeedbackView(View):
    def post(self, request):
        # Validate input
        if not request.is_ajax():
            return JsonResponse({"error": "Invalid request type"}, status=400)

        data = json.loads(request.body)
        customer_id = data.get("customer_id")
        feedback_text = data.get("feedback_text")
        feedback_date = data.get("feedback_date")

        if not all([customer_id, feedback_text]):
            return JsonResponse({"error": "Missing required fields"}, status=400)

        # Validate customer_id
        if not isinstance(customer_id, int):
            return JsonResponse({"error": "Customer ID must be an integer"}, status=400)

        # Validate feedback_text
        if not isinstance(feedback_text, str):
            return JsonResponse({"error": "Feedback text must be a string"}, status=400)

        # Validate feedback_date (optional)
        if feedback_date:
            try:
                feedback_date = datetime.strptime(feedback_date, "%Y-%m-%d")
            except ValueError:
                return JsonResponse({"error": "Invalid date format"}, status=400)

        # NLP sentiment analysis
        sentiment = nlp.polarity_scores(feedback_text)
        compound_score = sentiment["compound"]

        # Store in database
        try:
            with transaction.atomic():
                feedback = Feedback.objects.create(
                    customer_id=customer_id,
                    feedback_text=feedback_text,
                    feedback_date=feedback_date if feedback_date else datetime.now(),
                    sentiment_score=compound_score
                )
                cursor.execute("INSERT INTO feedback (customer_id, feedback_text, feedback_date, sentiment_score) VALUES (%s, %s, %s, %s)",
                               (customer_id, feedback_text, feedback_date, compound_score))
                db.commit()
                return JsonResponse({"status": "success", "data": model_to_dict(feedback)})
            cursor.close()
            db.close()
        except Exception as e:
            db.rollback()
            cursor.close()
            db.close()
            return JsonResponse({"error": str(e)}, status=500)

The following is the input: You are tasked with creating a Python package for simulating physical experiments, meant to be used by university physics departments. The package needs to manage different experiments, each characterized by its setup parameters (e.g., initial velocity, angle, mass) and results (e.g., distance traveled, time of flight). Implement the core class `Experiment` that encapsulates one physical experiment. It should have methods for setting up the experiment, running it using numpy for calculations, and storing the results in a structured format. Your package should also include functionalities for comparing different experiments based on their parameters or results.

```python
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

class Experiment:
    def __init__(self, setup_parameters):
        self.setup_parameters = setup_parameters
        self.results = {}

    def setup(self):
        # Example setup method
        self.results['initial_velocity'] = self.setup_parameters['initial_velocity']
        self.results['angle'] = self.setup_parameters['angle']
        self.results['mass'] = self.setup_parameters['mass']

    def run(self):
        # Example run method using numpy
        initial_velocity = self.results['initial_velocity']
        angle = self.results['angle']
        mass = self.results['mass']
        # Simple calculation for distance traveled (example)
        distance = initial_velocity ** 2 * np.sin(2 * angle * np.pi / 180) / (9.81)
        self.results['distance_traveled'] = distance
        self.results['time_of_flight'] = (2 * initial_velocity * np.sin(angle * np.pi / 180)) / 9.81

    def store_results(self, filename):
        df = pd.DataFrame(self.results)
        df.to_csv(filename, index=False)

    def compare_experiments(self, other_experiment):
        # Example comparison based on results
        if 'distance_traveled' in self.results and 'distance_traveled' in other_experiment.results:
            return self.results['distance_traveled'] > other_experiment.results['distance_traveled']
        return False
```

The following is the input: As part of an academic assignment in a database management course, you are required to design and implement a library management system using Python and SQLite. The system should include classes for `Book` and `Member`. The `Book` class should have properties like `isbn`, `title`, `author`, and `availability`. The `Member` class should include `member_id`, `name`, and `borrowed_books` (a list of books borrowed by the member). Implement functionality to add books, register members, borrow and return books, ensuring that all database transactions are properly handled. Code must be well-commented to explain each function and its purpose, aligning with educational goals of understanding database operations and object-oriented programming.

```python
import sqlite3
from datetime import datetime

# Create a database connection
def create_connection():
    conn = sqlite3.connect('library.db')
    return conn

# Create tables
def create_tables(conn):
    c = conn.cursor()
    c.execute('''CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS books
                 (isbn TEXT PRIMARY KEY, title TEXT, author TEXT, availability BOOLEAN)''')
    c.execute('''CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS members
                 (member_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, borrowed_books TEXT)''')
    conn.commit()

# Add a book to the database
def add_book(conn, isbn, title, author):
    c = conn.cursor()
    c.execute("INSERT INTO books (isbn, title, author, availability) VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?)",
              (isbn, title, author, True))
    conn.commit()

# Register a member
def register_member(conn, member_id, name):
    c = conn.cursor()
    c.execute("INSERT INTO members (member_id, name) VALUES (?, ?)", (member_id, name))
    conn.commit()

# Borrow a book
def borrow_book(conn, member_id, isbn):
    c = conn.cursor()
    c.execute("UPDATE books SET availability = FALSE WHERE isbn = ? AND availability = TRUE", (isbn,))
    c.execute("INSERT INTO members (member_id, borrowed_books) VALUES (?, ?)", (member_id, isbn))
    conn.commit()

# Return a book
def return_book(conn, member_id, isbn):
    c = conn.cursor()
    c.execute("UPDATE books SET availability = TRUE WHERE isbn = ? AND availability = FALSE", (isbn,))
    c.execute("DELETE FROM members WHERE member_id = ? AND borrowed_books = ?", (member_id, isbn))
    conn.commit()

# Get all books
def get_books(conn):
    c = conn.cursor()
    c.execute("SELECT * FROM books")
    return c.fetchall()

# Get all members
def get_members(conn):
    c = conn.cursor()
    c.execute("SELECT * FROM members")
    return c.fetchall()

# Main function to run the system
def main():
    conn = create_connection()
    create_tables(conn)
    
    # Example usage
    add_book(conn, "978-3-111111111-1", "Python Programming", "Mark Lutz")
    register_member(conn, 1, "John Doe")
    borrow_book(conn, 1, "978-3-111111111-1")
    return_book(conn, 1, "978-3-111111111-1")
    
    # Get and print books
    books = get_books(conn)
    print("Books in the library:")
    for book in books:
        print(f"ISBN: {book[0]}, Title: {book[1]}, Author: {book[2]}, Availability: {book[3]}")
    
    # Get and print members
    members = get_members(conn)
    print("\nMembers in the system:")
    for member in members:
        print(f"Member ID: {member[0]}, Name: {member[1]}, Borrowed Books: {member[2]}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

The following is the input: You are part of an open-source project developing a sports statistics application. The application keeps records of various sports. Your task is to integrate a new feature for capturing and storing cricket match statistics. Implement a module `cricketStats.py` that records the number of runs, wickets, and overs for a match. Ensure the module includes functionality to update stats during a live match, store end-of-match statistics in a SQLite database, and retrieve statistics for any given match. Follow the code style guidelines established in the project's documentation and add unit tests for your new feature.

```python
import sqlite3
from datetime import datetime

# cricketStats.py

def create_table():
    conn = sqlite3.connect('cricket_stats.db')
    c = conn.cursor()
    c.execute('''CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS matches
                 (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
                  match_date TEXT,
                  runs INTEGER,
                  wickets INTEGER,
                  overs REAL,
                  status TEXT)''')
    conn.commit()
    conn.close()

def insert_match(runs, wickets, overs, match_date, status='completed'):
    conn = sqlite3.connect('cricket_stats.db')
    c = conn.cursor()
    c.execute('''INSERT INTO matches (runs, wickets, overs, match_date, status)
                 VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?)''',
              (runs, wickets, overs, match_date, status))
    conn.commit()
    conn.close()

def get_match_stats(match_id):
    conn = sqlite3.connect('cricket_stats.db')
    c = conn.cursor()
    c.execute('''SELECT * FROM matches WHERE id = ?''', (match_id,))
    result = c.fetchone()
    conn.close()
    return result

def update_match_stats(match_id, runs, wickets, overs):
    conn = sqlite3.connect('cricket_stats.db')
    c = conn.cursor()
    c.execute('''UPDATE matches SET runs = ?, wickets = ?, overs = ?
                 WHERE id = ?''',
              (runs, wickets, overs, match_id))
    conn.commit()
    conn.close()
```

The following is the input: You are a data analyst working with environmental data to study climate change impacts. Use Python to create a script that reads temperature data from multiple CSV files ('temperature_data1.csv', 'temperature_data2.csv', etc.). Each file contains daily temperature readings with the columns 'Date' and 'Temperature'. Merge these files into a single DataFrame, calculate the average monthly temperatures, and plot this data using matplotlib. The plot should include labels for months, temperature scale, and a title indicating 'Average Monthly Temperatures'.

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Read temperature data from multiple CSV files
files = ['temperature_data1.csv', 'temperature_data2.csv', 'temperature_data3.csv']
df_list = [pd.read_csv(file) for file in files]

# Merge the DataFrames
df_merged = pd.concat(df_list, ignore_index=True)

# Calculate average monthly temperatures
df_merged['Month'] = pd.to_datetime(df_merged['Date']).dt.to_period('M')
df_avg = df_merged.groupby(['Month']).mean().reset_index()

# Plot the data
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
plt.plot(df_avg['Month'], df_avg['Temperature'], marker='o', linestyle='-', color='b')
plt.title('Average Monthly Temperatures')
plt.xlabel('Month')
plt.ylabel('Temperature (°C)')
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()

The following is the input: You are tasked with improving a bioinformatics analysis tool that processes large genomic datasets. Develop a Python function utilizing numpy and pandas to efficiently filter and process genetic mutation data from a CSV file named `mutations.csv`. The function should be able to identify mutations that meet specific frequency thresholds and categorize them by gene and mutation type. Finally, generate summary statistics of the categorized data, and save the results into a new CSV file `summary_mutations.csv`.

```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

def process_mutations():
    # Load the CSV file
    mutations_df = pd.read_csv('mutations.csv')
    
    # Filter mutations based on frequency thresholds
    # Assuming frequency threshold is 0.01 (1%)
    threshold = 0.01
    filtered_mutations = mutations_df[mutations_df['mutation_frequency'] > threshold]
    
    # Categorize mutations by gene and mutation type
    categorized_mutations = filtered_mutations.groupby(['gene', 'mutation_type']).size().reset_index(name='count')
    
    # Generate summary statistics
    summary_stats = categorized_mutations.groupby('gene').agg(
        total_mutations=('count', 'sum'),
        total_frequency=('mutation_frequency', 'sum'),
        average_frequency=('mutation_frequency', 'mean')
    )
    
    # Save the results to a new CSV file
    summary_stats.to_csv('summary_mutations.csv', index=False)

process_mutations()
```

The following is the input: You are designing an algorithm for a weather prediction system using Python. Your task is to accept historical weather data in the form of a CSV file named 'weather_data.csv'. The dataset contains columns such as "date", "temperature", "humidity", and "wind_speed". You are required to process this data using pandas to calculate the average temperature and maximum wind speed for each month. The results should then be visualized using Matplotlib as separate line graphs, one for average temperatures and another for maximum wind speeds, labeled with appropriate units. Additionally, ensure the visuals are clear and the axes are properly annotated for better understanding.

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Load the CSV file
df = pd.read_csv('weather_data.csv')

# Calculate average temperature and maximum wind speed per month
df['month'] = pd.to_datetime(df['date']).dt.to_period('M')
avg_temp = df.groupby('month')['temperature'].mean()
max_wind = df.groupby('month')['wind_speed'].max()

# Plot average temperature
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
avg_temp.plot(kind='line', label='Average Temperature')
plt.title('Average Temperature per Month')
plt.xlabel('Month')
plt.ylabel('Temperature (°C)')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()

# Plot maximum wind speed
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
max_wind.plot(kind='line', label='Maximum Wind Speed')
plt.title('Maximum Wind Speed per Month')
plt.xlabel('Month')
plt.ylabel('Wind Speed (m/s)')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()
